summary: The candidate, Josefa, designs a YouTube-like video streaming service, focusing on upload and viewing functionalities. She discusses functional and non-functional requirements, including high availability and eventual consistency. The design covers separate flows for content creation (upload service, metadata DB, blob storage, transcoding queue) and content consumption (streaming service, CDN). Key considerations include sharding strategies, adaptive bitrate streaming, and caching for high read traffic, especially during peak events. She also briefly touches on analytics and fault tolerance.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Great, thanks Josefa. We're going to be doing a system design interview like we mentioned, and this is what I'd like to ask: design a service like YouTube where users can upload and view videos.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: All right, let's get started. YouTube-like system. I have a few questions on it. Basically, with a YouTube-like system, there are two aspects: one is your content creators, and one is other people who are consuming the content. When we create a system like this, we need to be tailored to both sides. In terms of creating content, I'm assuming that users can upload in any format from any device, like a cell phone or a sophisticated camera, and then our system takes care of the post-processing.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, that's a good assumption.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: All right. On the consumption side, I think that we also have a similar use case where, given a video, a user should be able to view it on any device, be it an iPhone or Android device, a television, or a laptop. So the viewing experience should be device agnostic as well. Another thing we need to make sure of is whether we need to look at interaction on the videos, like commenting, likes, dislikes, up arrow, down arrow, as a part of this system.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Good question. Maybe we can leave that for V2 if we have time, but let's just focus on uploading and viewing for now.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: All right, these are two main functional requirements for the system. In terms of the non-functional requirements, I would say that for a system like this, high availability is very important. You want the system to be available, and in terms of consistency, I think we can target eventual consistency. The reason is that once a video is uploaded, it need not be available immediately or in real-time to all users. There can be a lag from the time the video is uploaded to the time the video is available. Once the video is available to the users, you want it to be highly available and with as low latency as possible. Given this, we can get into a high-level system. Basically, as I said, you have two streams of data flow: the write part, which is content creation, and the read part, which is content consumption. Like any video streaming application, the ratio of reads to writes will vary heavily in favor of reads. So there will be more people viewing the videos than videos getting uploaded into the system. Our system needs to account for that. Let me first, at a high level, figure out what our content creation system would look like, and then next, we would look at how the consumption would be. Let's say we start off with the client over here. The client is connected to your video upload service. You could have some kind of an upload video API. Basically, we would use some kind of an open socket connection, like WebRTC or something, so that a stream of video can be passed from the server to the client. The size of the video can vary quite a bit, so it's not going to be feasible to use something like a REST endpoint or a REST protocol to transmit the actual video. We could use that REST endpoint to transmit the metadata around the video, but for the actual video, we would use some kind of an open socket connection to do that. We'll get into those details a little later on.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: You mentioned a video uploading service here, and what are some of the calls that this API would have?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Fundamentally, at a high level, this API would handle two things. One is your upload video call. This would take in the metadata around the video, like user ID, title of the video, description, meta tags, timestamp, etc. And then you have the actual video itself. So you could say this is your metadata over here, and then the next one would be where you are actually uploading the video itself. So you want to break this out into two parts: one which is just for strings, and the other is the stream of video that you are sending to your video service.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Got it.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Once the video service has it, one of the things we've mentioned here is that we want the video to be available on multiple devices. So the idea here is that the user is uploading video in one format, and we need to do some post-processing, encoding, or transcoding on the video so that it's available in multiple formats. We can have some kind of a queuing system here where this takes in the raw video from your video recording service and processes it into different formats and resolutions that we can serve to the users. There are two things on the storage side that we need to account for here. One will be our database for the metadata. This could be a relational database that has user information, video metadata information. In the future, if we augment the system to have something like likes, comments, any interaction that we do with the video. The other storage we would have is some kind of a blob storage like S3 to store the actual videos. Anything coming from transcoding is going here. All metadata coming from the video service is going here directly.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: At scale, are you still going to be storing them like this, or would you partition them in any way?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: At scale, we would partition both databases. Another thing we would do at scale, from a content viewing side, is move this data into a CDN. We would not let users directly come to this blob storage. As our users scale, you could see this database getting sharded. In terms of video IDs, you could say a hash of the video IDs from certain ranges would land in every shard. Correspondingly, the same thing could happen to the blob storage as well.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Cool, got it. So, this is basically your upload client. Besides sharding by IDs, what are some other ideas you would have for sharding?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: You could shard videos by geography, by genre, what type of video it is, or by creator as well. These are some of the different ways you could shard them.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: And which one would you pick?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Given the global nature of something like YouTube, it would make sense to shard videos by users, like creators, in my view. Generally, people tend to follow certain creators or channels within their profile, and that's how a recommendation is created for them. So that would make querying easier for them. I would shard it on the users who are creating those videos.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Got it, yep.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So now this would be your user, somebody who's viewing the video. We would have a streaming service. As I've mentioned, the video streaming traffic is going to be way more as compared to your video upload. Both of them are going to be horizontally scaled, but the video streaming service is going to be scaled much more proportionally than your upload service.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, so basically this would be like a stream video URL. The input would be the URL of the video that you want to stream. One thing we could do is build out a CDN here first. Once a request comes to your video service, it would first need to check with the database whether that video exists, whether the user has the required permissions to view the video, and what not. Once that is validated, the video streaming service would then give the response from the CDN to the user. Now, how the video is getting to the CDN? It's getting from your blob storage to the CDN. There are a couple ways to get this video from the blob storage to the CDN. One is we actively, as a system, as the videos are getting processed and added into blob storage, we actively add them into CDN. Another way we do that is we first wait for a user to request a video. We check it in CDN. If it's not there, then we fetch it from the blob storage and put it onto the CDN. Basically, the first few users who would want to request the videos would have a slightly slower experience than subsequent users once the CDN has the particular video. The advantage of doing the second approach is that you would only put the most popular videos in CDN rather than putting everything, because from a cost standpoint, CDNs are going to be much more expensive than a blob storage like S3. You would eventually want to use some kind of an invalidation strategy on the CDN, like an LRU system, where older videos are eventually removed or invalidated from the CDN. One thing I would also want to mention on the database side, I could see that there are a couple main entities here. You have the user, and you could have your ID, email, name. The other very important entity here is the video itself. Again, you'll have the ID for the video, which will be a string, you can have the title, description, tags (which are comma-separated), timestamp, location, and much more. You could have URLs here. Basically, these URLs are the URLs of where the videos are stored in your blob storage. Given enough videos, or enough variations of your videos, you can think of splitting it out into a table of its own where a unique video ID maps to a unique location in URL. The other thing you could also do here when the user is streaming is having an adaptive bitrate system. As bytes are going from the CDN to the user, the user is sending feedback whether it's able to consume those bytes at a respectable pace or if the bytes are getting transferred at a respectable pace. Based on the data of how the bandwidth is for the user, we can up or down the resolution of what we are sending to the user. We can go down from, say, a 1080p to a 360p if needed. We keep monitoring it every few seconds to see whether we need to up or down the bitrate.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yep, that makes sense. So I see that you have a video streaming service here, and you've mentioned, which is true, that we're going to have more people reading than writing. What would happen if suddenly we have a big event like the Super Bowl, and many people are coming and the video streaming service fails?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So, are we talking about a live event here, or are we talking about some pre-uploaded video for a Super Bowl?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Let's say that we know that next week there's going to be a big event, and many people are coming to watch videos. What would you do?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: If that is the case, we definitely are scaling this system horizontally. The other thing you would want to do is also have an extra layer of cache in the middle. If you know that this video, or for example, as you said, some influencer posted a video and you have a rush of traffic towards that video, you don't want the video streaming service to get bombarded and overloaded. What you could essentially do is cache all of this information that it is getting from the DB in some kind of an in-memory cache and then respond back to the users much more quickly. You could build a cache here. The video streaming service first talks to the cache, and then the cache can talk to the database.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: And how would the cache be updated?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Caches can be updated. This information is the metadata of the videos itself. You could think of it that way: when the first request comes in, if the information is not in the cache, you update the cache. Then for every subsequent request, you use the cache. Or you proactively push this information into the cache. One way to think about it is every time the blob storage processing is complete, the blob storage could give that information to the DB, and the DB can also collectively write into the cache. Whatever information is in the cache is mainly going to be static by nature. It's not changing from an upload standpoint.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Cool, got it. Awesome, this is a good solution. Was there anything else you want to add?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: I think one thing you can look at here is analytics information as well. If you wanted to see what users are viewing, how many seconds, and all of that, you could have a stream or an analytics system here, which is constantly reading it in the background from here. Then you have a large data warehouse that you're pushing these events onto for further processing and visualization down the line.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Great, yep, that's a very important feature too. But I think for the functionality requirements that we have here, this is a good solution. So I'll take off my interviewer hat now. Thanks again for the great solution, Josefa. I have some feedback here that I'll share with you, and I'd also love if you can share some self-feedback for the audience as well. I really like the clarifying questions that you asked in the beginning. I think they were very thorough, and you did a good job checking in with me and leaving some space and not just rambling. So I think your communication was really good. You left some good time for me to check in with you and give you some follow-up questions as well. On my end, you also pointed out a very good trend which would happen, which is that we're going to have more reads than writes, and you were able to design around this expectation. You also mentioned some good edge cases to consider, for example, not everyone would have permission to watch every video. The majority of videos might be public, but there might be some private videos or unlisted videos too. So I think it's also good to show that you were considering these different edge cases. All in all, great system design. Thanks for your time and coming to share your knowledge with the audience, and I'd love for you to share some self-feedback too if you have any.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Sure, I think maybe one of the things I was thinking about is adding more fault tolerance into the system. How would we, outside of sharding, look at a master-slave kind of a configuration for our database? And even for our videos, given that this is a very data-heavy system, how do we optimize for space here if you're looking purely from a cost standpoint? Eventually, the numbers would add up given enough scale.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Got it, yep, great consideration. Thank you for your time, Josefa, and for the audience, good luck with your upcoming system design interview.

training_pairs.question: Do you mind just telling the audience a little bit about who you are?
training_pairs.answer: Sure, hey everyone, this is Josefa. I am an engineering manager at Wealthfront. Prior to that, I was at PayPal for about eight and a half years.

training_pairs.question: Design a service like YouTube where users can upload and view videos.
training_pairs.answer: All right, let's get started. YouTube-like system. I have a few questions on it. Basically, with a YouTube-like system, there are two aspects: one is your content creators, and one is other people who are consuming the content. When we create a system like this, we need to be tailored to both sides. In terms of creating content, I'm assuming that users can upload in any format from any device, like a cell phone or a sophisticated camera, and then our system takes care of the post-processing.

training_pairs.question: Do we need to look at interaction on the videos, like commenting, likes, dislikes, as a part of this system?
training_pairs.answer: Another thing we need to make sure of is whether we need to look at interaction on the videos, like commenting, likes, dislikes, up arrow, down arrow, as a part of this system.
training_pairs.follow_up: Good question. Maybe we can leave that for V2 if we have time, but let's just focus on uploading and viewing for now.

This would take in the metadata around the video, like user ID, title of the video, description, meta tags, timestamp, etc. And then you have the actual video itself. So you could say this is your metadata over here, and then the next one would be where you are actually uploading the video itself. So you want to break this out into two parts: one which is just for strings, and the other is the stream of video that you are sending to your video service.

training_pairs.answer: At scale, we would partition both databases. Another thing we would do at scale, from a content viewing side, is move this data into a CDN. We would not let users directly come to this blob storage. As our users scale, you could see this database getting sharded. In terms of video IDs, you could say a hash of the video IDs from certain ranges would land in every shard. Correspondingly, the same thing could happen to the blob storage as well.
training_pairs.follow_up: Cool, got it. So, this is basically your upload client. Besides sharding by IDs, what are some other ideas you would have for sharding?

training_pairs.question: Besides sharding by IDs, what are some other ideas you would have for sharding?
training_pairs.answer: You could shard videos by geography, by genre, what type of video it is, or by creator as well. These are some of the different ways you could shard them.
training_pairs.follow_up: And which one would you pick?

training_pairs.question: And which one would you pick?
training_pairs.answer: Given the global nature of something like YouTube, it would make sense to shard videos by users, like creators, in my view. Generally, people tend to follow certain creators or channels within their profile, and that's how a recommendation is created for them. So that would make querying easier for them. I would shard it on the users who are creating those videos.

training_pairs.question: What would happen if suddenly we have a big event like the Super Bowl, and many people are coming and the video streaming service fails?
training_pairs.answer: So, are we talking about a live event here, or are we talking about some pre-uploaded video for a Super Bowl?
training_pairs.follow_up: Let's say that we know that next week there's going to be a big event, and many people are coming to watch videos. What would you do?

training_pairs.question: Let's say that we know that next week there's going to be a big event, and many people are coming to watch videos. What would you do?
training_pairs.answer: If that is the case, we definitely are scaling this system horizontally. The other thing you would want to do is also have an extra layer of cache in the middle. If you know that this video, or for example, as you said, some influencer posted a video and you have a rush of traffic towards that video, you don't want the video streaming service to get bombarded and overloaded. What you could essentially do is cache all of this information that it is getting from the DB in some kind of an in-memory cache and then respond back to the users much more quickly. You could build a cache here. The video streaming service first talks to the cache, and then the cache can talk to the database.
training_pairs.follow_up: And how would the cache be updated?

training_pairs.question: And how would the cache be updated?
training_pairs.answer: Caches can be updated. This information is the metadata of the videos itself. You could think of it that way: when the first request comes in, if the information is not in the cache, you update the cache. Then for every subsequent request, you use the cache. One way to think about it is every time the blob storage processing is complete, the blob storage could give that information to the DB, and the DB can also collectively write into the cache. training_pairs.question: Was there anything else you want to add?
training_pairs.answer: I think one thing you can look at here is analytics information as well. If you wanted to see what users are viewing, how many seconds, and all of that, you could have a stream or an analytics system here, which is constantly reading it in the background from here. Then you have a large data warehouse that you're pushing these events onto for further processing and visualization down the line.

training_pairs.answer: Sure, I think maybe one of the things I was thinking about is adding more fault tolerance into the system. How would we, outside of sharding, look at a master-slave kind of a configuration for our database? And even for our videos, given that this is a very data-heavy system, how do we optimize for space here if you're looking purely from a cost standpoint? Eventually, the numbers would add up given enough scale.
